description: Physics grounding for the physics lens — mechanics, thermodynamics, E&M, quantum, relativity, statistical mechanics. Accurate first-principles reasoning fuel routed to its lens.

entries.title: Newton's laws & conservation
entries.summary: F=ma (with F=dp/dt the more general form) plus equal-and-opposite reaction give classical mechanics. Their deepest content is the conservation laws — momentum, angular momentum, and energy — which follow (Noether) from translational, rotational, and time symmetry. Conservation is the workhorse: collisions, orbits, and rigid-body/robot dynamics are solved by it before any differential equation.

entries.title: Lagrangian & Hamiltonian mechanics
entries.summary: Instead of forces, formulate mechanics from the action S=∫L dt with L=T−V; the Euler–Lagrange equations make the action stationary. The Hamiltonian H=T+V gives phase-space (q,p) dynamics. This generalized-coordinate view is how robot manipulator equations of motion are derived (and feeds feedback-linearization/computed-torque control) and is the gateway to quantum and field theory.

entries.title: Laws of thermodynamics
entries.summary: First law: energy is conserved (ΔU=Q−W). Second law: entropy of an isolated system never decreases — heat flows hot→cold and no engine beats the Carnot efficiency 1−T_c/T_h. Third law: entropy→0 as T→0. The second law sets the arrow of time and the ultimate limits on every engine, refrigerator, and computation (Landauer's bound).

entries.title: Statistical mechanics & the Boltzmann distribution
entries.summary: Macroscopic thermodynamics emerges from counting microstates: a state of energy E has probability ∝ e^(−E/kT) (Boltzmann), normalized by the partition function Z. Entropy is S=k ln Ω. This is where temperature, free energy, and phase transitions come from — and the same Boltzmann form reappears as the softmax/energy-based models in ML.

entries.title: Maxwell's equations & electromagnetism
entries.summary: Four equations (Gauss for E and B, Faraday, Ampère–Maxwell) unify electricity and magnetism and predict electromagnetic waves traveling at c — light. They give the Lorentz force F=q(E+v×B) that drives every motor and sensor, and their relativistic invariance is what motivated special relativity.

entries.title: Special relativity
entries.summary: Postulating that c is the same in every inertial frame forces time dilation, length contraction, and the relativity of simultaneity (Lorentz transformations), with E=mc² as energy–mass equivalence. Spacetime intervals, not separate space and time, are invariant. It is essential wherever speeds approach c — and GPS already needs both special and general relativistic corrections to stay accurate.

entries.title: General relativity
entries.summary: Gravity is not a force but the curvature of spacetime by mass-energy: Einstein's field equations Gμν=8πG Tμν relate curvature to matter, and free-falling bodies follow geodesics. It predicts gravitational time dilation, light bending, black holes, and gravitational waves — and the manifold/tangent-space geometry it runs on is the same machinery used elsewhere in the math lens.

entries.title: Quantum mechanics: the Schrödinger equation
entries.summary: A quantum state is a wavefunction ψ evolving by iℏ∂ψ/∂t=Ĥψ; |ψ|² gives measurement probabilities (Born rule). Observables are operators, superposition and entanglement have no classical analogue, and the uncertainty principle ΔxΔp≥ℏ/2 bounds simultaneous knowledge. This is the substrate of chemistry, semiconductors, and quantum computing.

entries.title: Waves, resonance & the harmonic oscillator
entries.summary: The simple harmonic oscillator (ẍ=−ω²x) is physics' most reused model — pendula, circuits, molecular bonds, and the modes of any system near equilibrium. Driving it near its natural frequency produces resonance (large response), the phenomenon behind musical instruments, bridge failures, and the frequency-response/Bode analysis of control systems.

entries.title: Fluid dynamics & the Navier–Stokes equations
entries.summary: The Navier–Stokes equations express momentum conservation for a viscous fluid. The Reynolds number (inertial/viscous forces) predicts the transition from smooth laminar to chaotic turbulent flow. Whether smooth solutions always exist in 3D is an open Millennium Problem — and turbulence remains the canonical example of deterministic equations with effectively unpredictable behavior.

entries.title: Nonlinear dynamics & chaos
entries.summary: Deterministic nonlinear systems can show sensitive dependence on initial conditions — the butterfly effect — quantified by positive Lyapunov exponents. Trajectories settle onto strange attractors with fractal structure. Chaos bounds long-range prediction (weather) and connects physics to the dynamical-systems view shared with control theory and the robotics lens.

entries.title: Dimensional analysis & scaling
entries.summary: Physical equations must be dimensionally consistent; the Buckingham-π theorem says a relation among n variables with k dimensions reduces to n−k dimensionless groups. This yields scaling laws (why small animals lose heat fast, why model-scale tests predict full-scale behavior) and is the fastest sanity check and order-of-magnitude estimation tool in all of physics and engineering.
